11: bayom hahu aqim et sukat David hanofelet vegadarta et pirzeihen vaharisotyav aqim uvenituha keyeme olam: 'In that day I will raise up the booth of David that is fallen and repair its breaches, and raise up its ruins and rebuild it as in the days of old.' The 'fallen booth of David' is an image of the Davidic dynasty in ruins — either the northern kingdom's secession (931 BCE) or the Babylonian destruction of the Davidic throne (586 BCE). James's citation at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:16-17) reads the restoration as the messianic reign of Jesus: the booth is raised; now the Gentiles ('all the nations who are called by my name', Amos 9:12 in the LXX, which differs from the MT) stream in. The LXX variant makes the Gentile-inclusion application cleaner; James uses the LXX version. This is not a mistaken quotation but a deliberate use of the LXX rendering that the Spirit has superintended to carry the intended typological meaning.
1: ra'iti et Adonai nitzav al-ha-mizbeach — "I saw the Lord standing beside the altar." The fifth and final vision is unique: YHWH is standing by the altar himself, not showing Amos a symbolic image. The command — "Strike the capital (kapitor) so that the thresholds shake" — is directed at the sanctuary itself. The sanctuary that was meant to be the meeting point of heaven and earth becomes the site of judgment. Kapitor (capital of a column) may also evoke Caphtor (Crete), the origin point of the Philistines — a further irony in a verse addressed to Israel at the Bethel sanctuary.
2: im yachteru bishol — "if they dig down to Sheol." The escape-impossibility litany: digging to the underworld, climbing to heaven, hiding on Carmel's summit, concealing at the bottom of the sea. Each potential refuge is foreclosed. The hand of YHWH reaches every cosmic zone — below, above, horizontal extremes. This is not a celebration of divine omnipresence but a judgment-announcement of inescapability. The language echoes Psalm 139, which celebrates the same divine omnipresence as comfort; here it is terror.
3: im ye-chave'u be-rosh ha-Karmel — "if they hide themselves on top of Carmel." The dense forests of Carmel were the most impenetrable terrain in Israel. Even there, YHWH will search and take them. If they go to the floor of the sea, he will command the serpent (nachash, the chaos-dragon associated with the deep) to bite them. Every hiding place, natural or mythological, is accessible.
4: ve-im yelku va-shevi lifnei oyvehem — "if they go into captivity before their enemies." Even exile — the final escape attempt — is not escape. YHWH will command the sword there. The final declaration reverses the Aaronic blessing: "I will set my eyes on them for harm and not for good." The divine face that was invoked to shine on Israel (Numbers 6:25) is now fixed on them in judgment.
5: va-Adonai YHWH ha-tzva'ot — "the Lord YHWH of hosts." The doxology (Amos 9:5-6) interrupts the judgment sequence with a cosmic hymn. YHWH who touches the earth so it melts, who builds his upper chambers in the heavens and founds his vault upon the earth — this is the One executing the judgment. The doxology answers the implied question: who could carry out such comprehensive judgment? The One who structured the cosmos.
6: ha-boneh ba-shamayim ma'alotav — "who builds his upper chambers in the heavens." The cosmic architecture imagery: YHWH's upper chambers (ma'alot, steps/stories) in the heavens, his vault founded upon the earth, calling for the waters of the sea and pouring them upon the face of the earth. YHWH shemo — "YHWH is his name" — closes the doxology with the divine name as the ultimate credential.
7: ha-lo chitziim atem li b'nei Yisrael — "Are you not like the Cushites to me, O people of Israel?" A radical challenge to election privilege. The Exodus was not Israel's unique credential — YHWH also brought up the Philistines from Caphtor and the Arameans from Kir. The implication is not that the Exodus was meaningless but that election does not guarantee immunity from judgment. Covenant relationship intensifies accountability rather than suspending it.
8: hineh einei Adonai YHWH ba-mamlakhah ha-chata'ah — "the eyes of the Lord YHWH are upon the sinful kingdom." The distinction between nation and remnant appears: the sinful kingdom (institutional Israel as a political entity) will be destroyed, but YHWH will "not utterly destroy the house of Jacob." The two clauses hold judgment and preservation in tension — total dissolution of the present political structure, but not total extinction of the covenanted people.
9: ki hineh anochi metzaveh — "for I am commanding." The sifting (va-nifoti) metaphor: Israel shaken among the nations like grain in a sieve (kebara), but no pebble (tzror) will fall to the ground. The metaphor is complex: the sieve retains the stones (the guilty who will be destroyed) while the grain (the righteous remnant) passes through to safety. The sifting is the mechanism of judgment that simultaneously preserves.
10: ba-cherev yamutu kol chata'ei ami — "all the sinners of my people shall die by the sword." The criterion for inclusion in judgment: those who say "Evil shall not overtake or confront us." The self-exemption from judgment is itself the defining sin of the complacent (cf. Amos 6:3). Those who refused to take the covenant lawsuit seriously will find it executed against them.
12: le-ma'an yireshu et she'erit Edom — "that they may possess the remnant of Edom." The MT reads Edom; the LXX reads anthropon (humanity/Adam). Acts 15:17 follows the LXX in James's speech, citing this verse to support Gentile inclusion in the restored people. The divergence is theologically generative: MT promises Israel's possession of neighboring territory; LXX promises the nations (all of humanity) seeking the Lord. The NT application uses the LXX's universalizing reading as scriptural grounding for the Gentile mission.
13: hineh yamim ba'im — "behold, days are coming." The eschatological reversal: the plowman overtakes the reaper, the grape-treader overtakes the one who sows seed. Agriculture in reverse — the cycle so accelerated that harvesting and planting overlap. Mountains dripping new wine (assis, grape juice), hills flowing with it — the language of superabundant blessing that inverts the drought and famine of the judgment oracles.
14: ve-shavti et shevut ami Yisrael — "I will restore the fortunes of my people Israel." Shavti et shevut is the classic restoration formula — often translated "restore the captivity/fortunes" but functioning as an idiom for comprehensive reversal of all that has gone wrong. They will build ruined cities and inhabit them, plant vineyards and drink their wine, make gardens and eat their fruit. The specific activities named reverse the dispossession and exile that judgment brought.
15: u-netativ al adamatam — "I will plant them on their land." The closing image is rooted and permanent: "they shall never again be uprooted from the land that I have given them." The verb nata (plant) echoes the planting of a vine or tree — permanent, rooted, not subject to exile. The final word of Amos is not judgment but the covenant fidelity of YHWH who planted Israel and will not permit permanent uprooting. YHWH Eloheicha amar — "says the LORD your God" — the covenant formula as final seal.
